skill.name: semantic-scholar-library-feed
skill.description: Work with a user's Semantic Scholar account to read Research Feeds, inspect private Library folders, add papers to folders, and resolve Semantic Scholar paper records from identifiers such as arXiv IDs.
skill.summary: Manage Semantic Scholar research feeds and library folders via CLI with cookie-based authentication.
skill.author: zjsxply

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate research management tool for Semantic Scholar. Static findings flagged 185 potential issues, but manual evaluation confirms all are false positives. The skill uses Python CLI scripts for cookie-based API access, standard file paths (~/.auth/), and base64 encoding for decoding server-side rendered data. No malicious patterns, data exfiltration, or unauthorized credential usage detected.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged base64.b64decode as 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. Base64 is encoding, not encryption, and is used legitimately to decode SSR data from Semantic Scholar's HTML pages. This is standard API behavior.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Base64 decoding is clearly used for decoding var DATA from HTML SSR content, not for cryptographic purposes. Confirmed by context at decode_var_data function.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer detected 'backtick execution' in markdown documentation files (SKILL.md, references/*.md). Backticks in markdown are code fences, not shell commands. The actual Python scripts (semantic_scholar_cli.py, ss_store.py) use proper subprocess calls, not shell backticks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed via file inspection: all backticks appear in markdown code blocks (documentation), not actual executable code.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged file existence checks and URL parsing as 'system reconnaissance'. These are standard CLI operations: checking if cookie store exists, parsing folder IDs from URLs, validating API responses. Legitimate tool behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed via file inspection: file checks are for cookie store validation, URL parsing extracts folder IDs and windowUTC parameters for API calls.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hidden File Access
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged ~/.auth/ directory access as 'hidden file in home directory'. This is standard configuration directory for storing authentication tokens. No malicious intent detected.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: ~/.auth/ is a standard convention for storing authentication credentials. The cookie files are user-provided Semantic Scholar session tokens, not hardcoded secrets.

low_findings.title: Intentional: Temp Directory Access
low_findings.description: Skill writes to /tmp/ for intermediate data (research feed exports, folder exports). This is intentional for data processing workflows and standard practice for CLI tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed via file inspection: /tmp/ paths are user-provided output locations and curl import targets, not malicious staging areas.

content.user_title: Manage Semantic Scholar Research Feeds and Library Folders
content.value_statement: Researchers waste hours manually navigating Semantic Scholar's web interface to track papers. This skill automates feed export, folder management, and paper ID resolution through a CLI with cookie-based authentication.
actual_capabilities: Read and export Research Feeds from authenticated Semantic Scholar accounts with pagination support
Inspect private Library folders to list paper entries and metadata
Add papers to Library folders via bulk API operations
Resolve paper records from arXiv IDs, DOIs, and Corpus identifiers using the Graph API
Decode server-side rendered data from Semantic Scholar web pages
Import cookies from browser-copied curl commands for authentication
limitations: Requires user to manually copy authenticated cookie data from browser DevTools
Cannot perform browser-based login; cookies must be provided via curl import
Limited to API endpoints; cannot access features not exposed through Semantic Scholar API

use_cases.title: Export Research Feed to JSON
use_cases.description: Researcher exports their daily paper recommendations from Semantic Scholar to a local JSON file for offline analysis and filtering.
use_cases.target_user: Academic researcher tracking new papers in their field

use_cases.title: Sync BibTeX Library to Folders
use_cases.description: Grad student compares their BibTeX collection against Semantic Scholar folders and adds missing papers automatically.

use_cases.title: Batch Resolve arXiv IDs
use_cases.description: Research assistant converts a list of arXiv preprint IDs to full Semantic Scholar paper records with metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Check cookie health
prompt_templates.prompt: Check if the Semantic Scholar cookie store is configured and valid by running the cookie-summary command.
prompt_templates.scenario: Before accessing private research feeds or library folders

prompt_templates.title: Import browser cookies
prompt_templates.prompt: Import cookies from a curl command. The user has copied a browser request from Semantic Scholar DevTools. Parse the curl and save the cookie bundle.
prompt_templates.scenario: When the cookie store is missing or stale

prompt_templates.title: Export research feed
prompt_templates.prompt: Crawl the Semantic Scholar research feed starting from the current window. Save results to /tmp/research-feed.json after each window to avoid losing progress.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve paper IDs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Graph API to resolve the following arXiv IDs to Semantic Scholar paper records: ARXIV:2602.08234, ARXIV:2602.12670. Return paperId, title, year, and externalIds for each.
prompt_templates.scenario: Batch converting identifiers for literature review

output_examples.input: Check the current research feed from Semantic Scholar
output_examples.output: Found 47 papers across 3 days in the current window. New papers: 'AgentArk: Distilling Multi-Agent Intelligence into a Single LLM Agent' (2024), 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models' (2023). 5 papers from your Machine Learning folder, 3 from Computer Vision.

output_examples.input: List papers in my Library folder
output_examples.output: Folder 'Research' contains 156 papers. Most recent additions: 'Attention Is All You Need' (added 2024-01-15), 'BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers' (added 2024-01-14). Sorted by date added to library.

output_examples.input: Add this paper to my reading list
output_examples.output: Successfully added 'Attention Is All You Need' to folder 'Reading List' (ID: 13895811). Paper ID: a5f3c6a51bbee0f1d12abf3a3c7e5b9d. The paper is now visible in your Semantic Scholar library.

best_practices: Import cookies from a successful authenticated request to ensure session validity
Persist feed crawl results after each window to avoid losing progress on interruption
Use paperId for deduplication instead of titles, as titles may have slight variations
anti_patterns: Do not use Playwright for login; import cookies from browser DevTools instead
Do not trust title-based search alone; prefer stable identifiers like arXiv IDs
Do not request pageSize > 100 as it returns 400 error from Semantic Scholar API

faq.question: How do I get cookies for authentication?
faq.answer: Open Semantic Scholar in your browser, go to DevTools (F12), Network tab, refresh the page, find any authenticated request, right-click and 'Copy as cURL (bash)'. Then use import-curl command.

faq.question: What cookies are required?
faq.answer: At minimum, you need 'sid' and 's2' cookies. Without both, private library and feed access returns 401 errors.

faq.question: Why does the skill write to /tmp/?
faq.answer: The skill writes feed crawls and folder exports to /tmp/ as intermediate data for processing. You can specify different output paths with --output flag.

faq.question: How do I resume a feed crawl?
faq.answer: Pass --resume flag with the same --output file path. The skill reads the previous state and continues from the last window.

faq.question: Can I add papers to multiple folders at once?
faq.answer: Yes, pass multiple --folder-ids arguments. The folder-add command supports adding to multiple folders in one request.

faq.question: How do I find my folder ID?
faq.answer: Use ssr-dump --list-names to see available snapshots, or inspect the folder URL in Semantic Scholar web interface. Folder IDs appear in URLs like /me/library/folder/13895811.
